title: Google is not losing the AI race, Pichai tells doubters as cloud jumps 82%
excerpt: Sundar Pichai spent Alphabet’s Wednesday earnings call doing something he is not used to doing: arguing that Google is not, in fact, falling behind in artificial intelligence. Analysts wanted to know why the next Gemini model had slipped, and whether the company had quietly ceded the frontier to OpenAI and Anthropic. “We’ve had clearly frontier […]
content_html:
Sundar Pichai spent Alphabet’s Wednesday earnings call doing something he is not used to doing: arguing that Google is not, in fact, falling behind in artificial intelligence.

Analysts wanted to know why the next Gemini model had slipped, and whether the company had quietly ceded the frontier to OpenAI and Anthropic.

“We’ve had clearly frontier models,” Pichai said on the call. “There are many attributes on which we are still at the frontier; there are areas where we’ve acknowledged we need to improve and coding and agentic coding is an example of that.”

For a company that likes to remind everyone it invented the transformer, the admission was unusually candid.

The remarks answered a run of pointed questions from JPMorgan’s Doug Anmuth and Barclays’ Ross Sandler, who pressed him on a delayed Gemini Pro and a release schedule that has trailed rivals.

Google’s next Pro model is months behind schedule, and coding is exactly the ground on which OpenAI’s Codex and Anthropic’s Claude have been peeling off developers.

Pichai’s answer was to promise more, and sooner. He said Google had begun its most ambitious pre-training run yet for Gemini 4, told analysts “I think people will be pleased” when it lands, and signalled that future models would arrive at close to a monthly cadence.

He also leaned on demand as evidence that the doubters had it wrong. Google remains “supply constrained,” Pichai said, which he cast as a sign of momentum rather than strain.

Gemini models now process 22 billion API tokens a minute, and nearly 500 cloud customers have each run more than a trillion tokens over the past year.

The figures underneath the argument were strong. Alphabet posted revenue of $119.8 billion for the quarter, up 24% on a year earlier, while Google Cloud grew 82% to $24.8 billion, comfortably ahead of the roughly 64% analysts had pencilled in. Cloud backlog reached $514 billion.

Advertising, still the engine, did its share alongside cloud. YouTube brought in $11.06 billion in ad revenue, up from $9.79 billion a year earlier, and the wider advertising business kept growing even as search shifted towards AI-generated answers.

Consumer numbers held up too. The Gemini app hit 950 million monthly active users, its daily total tripling over the past year, and nearly 90% of the Fortune 100 now run Gemini Enterprise.

Google’s full-stack bet, from custom chips to models to agents, is the version of events Pichai would like investors to read.

Net income came in at a startling $112.1 billion, though most of that was an accounting mirage. Around $80 billion of it was an unrealised gain on Alphabet’s stake of roughly 14% in Anthropic, whose valuation tripled during the quarter.

What unsettled the market was spending. Alphabet lifted its 2026 capital expenditure guidance by about $15 billion, to between $195 billion and $205 billion, extending a year of record AI capex across Big Tech, and management signalled the bill would climb again in 2027.

The message to investors was that the constraint is chips and data centres, not demand, and that Google intends to keep buying its way to the front.

Wall Street was not fully persuaded. Shares fell more than 3% after hours, and are down roughly 9% since late April on the Gemini delays and a string of senior departures.

The anxiety is not new. The next Gemini Pro was reportedly expected in June, and its slip has coincided with a run of departures from Google’s AI ranks, feeding a story in which the company that published the transformer paper keeps struggling to turn research into shipped product.

The concession on coding was the tell. A year ago Google was beating estimates and closing on Nvidia as the most valuable company in the world; now its chief executive is answering questions about whether it can keep pace. The next Gemini, whenever it ships, will have to make his case for him.
content_text: Sundar Pichai spent Alphabet’s Wednesday earnings call doing something he is not used to doing: arguing that Google is not, in fact, falling behind in artificial intelligence. Analysts wanted to know why the next Gemini model had slipped, and whether the company had quietly ceded the frontier to OpenAI and Anthropic. “We’ve had clearly frontier models,” Pichai said on the call. “There are many attributes on which we are still at the frontier; there are areas where we’ve acknowledged we need to improve and coding and agentic coding is an example of that.” For a company that likes to remind everyone it invented the transformer, the admission was unusually candid. The remarks answered a run of pointed questions from JPMorgan’s Doug Anmuth and Barclays’ Ross Sandler, who pressed him on a delayed Gemini Pro and a release schedule that has trailed rivals. Google’s next Pro model is months behind schedule, and coding is exactly the ground on which OpenAI’s Codex and Anthropic’s Claude have been peeling off developers. Pichai’s answer was to promise more, and sooner. He said Google had begun its most ambitious pre-training run yet for Gemini 4, told analysts “I think people will be pleased” when it lands, and signalled that future models would arrive at close to a monthly cadence. He also leaned on demand as evidence that the doubters had it wrong. Google remains “supply constrained,” Pichai said, which he cast as a sign of momentum rather than strain. Gemini models now process 22 billion API tokens a minute, and nearly 500 cloud customers have each run more than a trillion tokens over the past year. The figures underneath the argument were strong. Alphabet posted revenue of $119.8 billion for the quarter, up 24% on a year earlier, while Google Cloud grew 82% to $24.8 billion, comfortably ahead of the roughly 64% analysts had pencilled in. Cloud backlog reached $514 billion. Advertising, still the engine, did its share alongside cloud. YouTube brought in $11.06 billion in ad revenue, up from $9.79 billion a year earlier, and the wider advertising business kept growing even as search shifted towards AI-generated answers. Consumer numbers held up too. The Gemini app hit 950 million monthly active users, its daily total tripling over the past year, and nearly 90% of the Fortune 100 now run Gemini Enterprise. Google’s full-stack bet, from custom chips to models to agents, is the version of events Pichai would like investors to read. Net income came in at a startling $112.1 billion, though most of that was an accounting mirage. Around $80 billion of it was an unrealised gain on Alphabet’s stake of roughly 14% in Anthropic, whose valuation tripled during the quarter. What unsettled the market was spending. Alphabet lifted its 2026 capital expenditure guidance by about $15 billion, to between $195 billion and $205 billion, extending a year of record AI capex across Big Tech, and management signalled the bill would climb again in 2027. The message to investors was that the constraint is chips and data centres, not demand, and that Google intends to keep buying its way to the front. Wall Street was not fully persuaded. Shares fell more than 3% after hours, and are down roughly 9% since late April on the Gemini delays and a string of senior departures. The anxiety is not new. The next Gemini Pro was reportedly expected in June, and its slip has coincided with a run of departures from Google’s AI ranks, feeding a story in which the company that published the transformer paper keeps struggling to turn research into shipped product. The concession on coding was the tell. A year ago Google was beating estimates and closing on Nvidia as the most valuable company in the world; now its chief executive is answering questions about whether it can keep pace. The next Gemini, whenever it ships, will have to make his case for him.

sentences.en: The anxiety is not new.
sentences.zh: 这种焦虑并不新鲜。

sentences.en: The next Gemini Pro was reportedly expected in June, and its slip has coincided with a run of departures from Google’s AI ranks, feeding a story in which the company that published the transformer paper keeps struggling to turn research into shipped product.
sentences.zh: 据报道，下一代 Gemini Pro 预计将在 6 月发布，而它的下滑恰逢谷歌 AI 队伍的一系列退出，这引发了一个故事，即发表 Transformer 论文的公司一直在努力将研究成果转化为交付的产品。

sentences.en: The concession on coding was the tell.
sentences.zh: 对编码的让步就是答案。

sentences.en: A year ago Google was beating estimates and closing on Nvidia as the most valuable company in the world; now its chief executive is answering questions about whether it can keep pace.
sentences.zh: 一年前，谷歌超出了预期，超越了英伟达，成为全球最有价值的公司；现在，其首席执行官正在回答有关其能否跟上步伐的问题。

sentences.en: The next Gemini, whenever it ships, will have to make his case for him.
sentences.zh: 下一个双子座，无论什么时候发布，都必须为他辩护。

content_html_zh:
桑达尔·皮查伊 (Sundar Pichai) 在 Alphabet 周三的财报电话会议上做了一些他不习惯做的事情：认为谷歌实际上并没有在人工智能方面落后。

分析师想知道为什么下一个 Gemini 模型会下滑，以及该公司是否已经悄悄地将前沿让给了 OpenAI 和 Anthropic。

“我们已经有了明显的前沿模型，”皮查伊在电话中说。 “我们在很多方面仍处于前沿；我们承认在某些领域需要改进，编码和代理编码就是一个例子。”

对于一家喜欢提醒每个人它发明了变压器的公司来说，这种承认是异常坦诚的。

这些言论回答了摩根大通的 Doug Anmuth 和巴克莱银行的 Ross Sandler 提出的一系列尖锐问题，他们向他施压，要求推迟 Gemini Pro 的发布以及落后于竞争对手的发布时间表。

谷歌的下一个 Pro 模型比计划晚了几个月，而编码正是 OpenAI 的 Codex 和 Anthropic 的 Claude 一直在剥削开发者的基础。

皮查伊的回答是承诺更多、更快。 他表示，谷歌已经开始为 Gemini 4 进行迄今为止最雄心勃勃的预训练，并告诉分析师“我认为人们会在它落地时感到高兴”，并表示未来的模型将接近每月一次的节奏。

他还以需求为证据，证明怀疑者的观点是错误的。 皮查伊表示，谷歌仍然“供应有限”，他认为这是势头而不是紧张的迹象。

Gemini 模型现在每分钟处理 220 亿个 API 令牌，近 500 个云客户在过去一年中每人运行了超过一万亿个令牌。

争论背后的数字是强有力的。 Alphabet 本季度营收为 1198 亿美元，同比增长 24%，而谷歌云增长 82%，达到 248 亿美元，远远高于分析师预测的约 64%。 云积压订单达到 5140 亿美元。

广告仍然是引擎，与云一起发挥了作用。 YouTube 带来了 110.6 亿美元的广告收入，高于一年前的 97.9 亿美元，而且即使搜索转向人工智能生成的答案，更广泛的广告业务仍在持续增长。

消费者数量也保持不变。 Gemini 应用程序的月活跃用户数达到 9.5 亿，比去年增加了两倍，财富 100 强中近 90% 的企业现在都在运行 Gemini Enterprise。

谷歌的全栈赌注，从定制芯片到模型再到代理，是皮查伊希望投资者阅读的事件版本。

净利润达到惊人的 1121 亿美元，尽管其中大部分只是会计上的幻象。 其中约 800 亿美元是 Alphabet 所持 Anthropic 约 14% 股份的未实现收益，该公司的估值在本季度增长了两倍。

令市场不安的是支出。 Alphabet 将 2026 年资本支出指引上调约 150 亿美元，达到 1950 亿至 2050 亿美元，延续了大型科技公司 AI 资本支出创纪录的一年，管理层表示该支出将在 2027 年再次攀升。

向投资者传达的信息是，限制因素是芯片和数据中心，而不是需求，谷歌打算继续通过收购来占据领先地位。

华尔街并没有完全被说服。 盘后股价下跌超过 3%，自 4 月底以来，由于 Gemini 航班延误和一系列高管离职，股价已下跌约 9%。

这种焦虑并不新鲜。 据报道，下一代 Gemini Pro 预计将在 6 月发布，而它的下滑恰逢谷歌 AI 队伍的一系列退出，这引发了一个故事，即发表 Transformer 论文的公司一直在努力将研究成果转化为交付的产品。

对编码的让步就是答案。 一年前，谷歌超出了预期，超越了英伟达，成为全球最有价值的公司；现在，其首席执行官正在回答有关其能否跟上步伐的问题。 下一个双子座，无论什么时候发布，都必须为他辩护。
